  • Hide All Code

Section 12: Clustering

Pablo Arés Gastesi

2026-07-07

1 Introduction: supervised vs. unsupervised learning

Every method in this course so far has been supervised: our data came with a known “result” — a number to predict (regression), or a class label (logistic regression, LDA/QDA, trees, KNN). That known result is what makes validation possible: split the data into training and test sets, fit on the training set, and check predictions against the known answers in the test set, using an MSE, a misclassification rate, or a confusion matrix.

Unsupervised learning has no such answer key. K-Means (Section 11) was our first example of it: there is no response variable, only predictors, and the goal is to describe structure in the predictors themselves — in that case, by partitioning observations into a fixed number of clusters. Clustering is the same kind of problem approached differently: group observations that are “similar” to each other, with no predefined groups to check against. It is a bit like handing someone a pile of photographs and asking them to sort it into similar-looking piles — one person might group by “nature vs. city”, another by “has a person in it or not.” There is no single right answer, and consequently no test set that can tell us whether the algorithm “got it right.”

Clustering is used, among other things, to group similar customers, similar documents (a step in natural language processing pipelines), or similar images. Section 11 covered K-Means, which requires the number of clusters \(K\) to be fixed in advance. Here we cover the other major family, hierarchical (agglomerative) clustering, which does not need \(K\) fixed ahead of time and produces a nice visual summary: the dendrogram. PCA (Section 13) rounds out the unsupervised methods in this course.

2 Measuring similarity

Hierarchical clustering needs two ingredients: a way to measure the distance between individual points, and a way to measure the distance between clusters of points (once clusters start forming). It assumes all variables are numeric with no missing values.

2.1 Distance between points

The two most common choices are the Euclidean distance (ordinary straight-line distance) and the Manhattan distance (sum of absolute coordinate differences — like walking along a city grid). For two points \(p_1 = (x_1, y_1)\) and \(p_2 = (x_2, y_2)\):

\[d_{\text{Euclidean}}(p_1, p_2) = \sqrt{(x_1-x_2)^2 + (y_1-y_2)^2}, \qquad d_{\text{Manhattan}}(p_1, p_2) = |x_1-x_2| + |y_1-y_2|\]

For \(p_1 = (0,0)\) and \(p_2 = (3,4)\):

p1 <- c(0, 0); p2 <- c(3, 4)
cat("Euclidean distance:", sqrt(sum((p1 - p2)^2)), "\n")
## Euclidean distance: 5
cat("Manhattan distance:", sum(abs(p1 - p2)), "\n")
## Manhattan distance: 7

2.2 Distance between clusters: linkage

Once a cluster contains more than one point, “distance to that cluster” needs its own definition — this choice is called the linkage method:

  • Complete (maximum) linkage: the largest distance between any pair of points, one from each cluster.
  • Single (minimum) linkage: the smallest such distance.
  • Average linkage: the mean of all pairwise distances between the two clusters.
  • Centroid linkage: the distance between the two clusters’ centroids (means).

Let’s compute all four by hand for two toy clusters: \(C_1 = \{P_1=(0,0), P_2=(2,2)\}\) and \(C_2 = \{P_3=(5,0), P_4=(5,5)\}\).

dist_fun <- function(x1, y1, x2, y2) sqrt((x1 - x2)^2 + (y1 - y2)^2)

d13 <- dist_fun(0, 0, 5, 0)
d14 <- dist_fun(0, 0, 5, 5)
d23 <- dist_fun(2, 2, 5, 0)
d24 <- dist_fun(2, 2, 5, 5)

cat("Point-to-point distances:", round(c(d13, d14, d23, d24), 4), "\n\n")
## Point-to-point distances: 5 7.0711 3.6056 4.2426
cat("Complete (max) linkage: ", max(d13, d14, d23, d24), "\n")
## Complete (max) linkage:  7.071068
cat("Single (min) linkage:   ", min(d13, d14, d23, d24), "\n")
## Single (min) linkage:    3.605551
cat("Average linkage:        ", mean(c(d13, d14, d23, d24)), "\n")
## Average linkage:         4.979815
c1_centroid <- c(1, 1)     # mean of P1, P2
c2_centroid <- c(5, 2.5)   # mean of P3, P4
cat("Centroid linkage:       ", dist_fun(c1_centroid[1], c1_centroid[2],
                                        c2_centroid[1], c2_centroid[2]), "\n")
## Centroid linkage:        4.272002

The four linkage rules give four different numbers for “the” distance between the same two clusters — and, as we will see, they can occasionally produce different dendrograms altogether.

3 A graphical example

Consider six labelled points:

datos_ejemplo <- data.frame(x = c(1, 2, 4, 6, 6, 6.5),
                           y = c(4, 4.5, 2, 4.5, 1.5, 2),
                           label = c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E", "F"))

ggplot(datos_ejemplo, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_label(nudge_y = 0.25) +
  expand_limits(x = c(1, 6), y = c(1, 5)) +
  theme_minimal()

Figure 3.1: Six points to be clustered

dist() computes every pairwise distance at once:

dist(datos_ejemplo[, 1:2])
##           1         2         3         4         5
## 2 1.1180340                                        
## 3 3.6055513 3.2015621                              
## 4 5.0249378 4.0000000 3.2015621                    
## 5 5.5901699 5.0000000 2.0615528 3.0000000          
## 6 5.8523500 5.1478151 2.5000000 2.5495098 0.7071068

The smallest distance is between points E and F, so they are merged into the first cluster: \(\{A, B, C, D, \{E,F\}\}\). Agglomerative clustering just repeats this — always merging whatever is currently closest — until everything is in one cluster. The dendrogram is the visual record of that process:

plot(hclust(dist(datos_ejemplo[, 1:2]), method = "average"),
    labels = datos_ejemplo$label, xlab = "", sub = "", ylab = "")

Figure 3.2: Dendrogram, average linkage

Reading from the bottom: E and F merge first, then A and B merge separately, then C joins the \(\{E,F\}\) cluster, then D joins that enlarged \(\{C,E,F\}\) cluster, and finally the two remaining branches (\(\{A,B\}\) and \(\{C,D,E,F\}\)) merge into one. A note of warning: the height on the left axis does not measure the distance between the final clusters — it only marks at what point each merge happened.

A dendrogram is a nested list, and R lets you drill into it directly. For example, the following extracts the first branch, then the first leaf within that branch:

my_cluster <- hclust(dist(datos_ejemplo[, 1:2]), method = "complete")
as.dendrogram(my_cluster)
## 'dendrogram' with 2 branches and 6 members total, at height 5.85235
as.dendrogram(my_cluster)[[1]]
## 'dendrogram' with 2 branches and 2 members total, at height 1.118034
as.dendrogram(my_cluster)[[1]][[1]]
## 'dendrogram' leaf '1', at height 0

3.1 Does the linkage method matter?

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
for (m in c("average", "single", "complete", "centroid")) {
  plot(hclust(dist(datos_ejemplo[, 1:2]), method = m),
      labels = datos_ejemplo$label, xlab = "", sub = "", ylab = "",
      main = paste(tools::toTitleCase(m), "linkage"))
}

Figure 3.3: The four linkage methods on the same data 

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

Here all four methods agree, because the points happen to be well separated into two obvious groups. This will not always be true — linkage choice can and does change the resulting clusters, particularly with noisier or less clearly separated data.

4 Scaling

As with every other distance-based method in this course (KNN in Section 10, K-Means in Section 11, PCA in Section 13), variables measured in very different units can dominate the distance calculation purely because of their scale, not because they are more informative. Standardising with scale() before clustering removes this artefact.

datos_scaled <- data.frame(x = scale(datos_ejemplo$x), y = scale(datos_ejemplo$y),
                           label = datos_ejemplo$label)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(hclust(dist(datos_ejemplo[, 1:2]), method = "complete"),
    labels = datos_ejemplo$label, xlab = "", sub = "", ylab = "", main = "Non-scaled")
plot(hclust(dist(datos_scaled[, 1:2]), method = "complete"),
    labels = datos_scaled$label, xlab = "", sub = "", ylab = "", main = "Scaled")

Figure 4.1: Complete linkage before and after scaling 

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

Here \(x\) and \(y\) were already on comparable scales, so scaling barely changes anything — but this cannot be assumed in general, and should always be checked (or simply applied by default) before clustering real data with variables in different units, as in the next example.

5 Clustering a real dataset: mtcars

We cluster cars using three variables: mpg, cyl, and disp (fuel economy, number of cylinders, engine displacement) — all on very different scales.

mtcars %>%
  select(mpg, cyl, disp) %>%
  dist() %>%
  hclust() %>%
  as.dendrogram() -> mtcars_dend_raw

par(mar = c(7, 3, 1, 1))
plot(mtcars_dend_raw)

Figure 5.1: mtcars clustered on raw (non-scaled) mpg/cyl/disp 

mtcars %>%
  select(mpg, cyl, disp) %>%
  scale() %>%
  dist() %>%
  hclust() %>%
  as.dendrogram() -> mtcars_dend

par(mar = c(7, 3, 1, 1))
plot(mtcars_dend)

Figure 5.2: mtcars clustered on standardised mpg/cyl/disp

5.1 Checking a merge by hand

The two cars merged first (rightmost pair) are the Merc 230 and Merc 240D. Since car names are the row names of mtcars, we can pull out their standardised values directly:

mtcars %>%
  select(mpg, cyl, disp) %>%
  scale() -> mtcars_scaled

mtcars_scaled[c("Merc 240D", "Merc 230"), ]
##                 mpg       cyl       disp
## Merc 240D 0.7150178 -1.224858 -0.6779309
## Merc 230  0.4495434 -1.224858 -0.7255351

Both cars have identical (standardised) cyl and disp, and fairly close mpg — hence they are each other’s nearest neighbour and merge first.

5.2 Does the clustering line up with another variable?

It’s natural to ask whether the clusters correspond to some variable we didn’t include — here, transmission type (am: automatic vs. manual).

my_colors <- ifelse(mtcars$am == 0, "forestgreen", "green")

par(mar = c(10, 1, 1, 1))
mtcars_dend %>%
  set("labels_col", value = c("blue", "orange", "grey"), k = 3) %>%
  set("branches_k_color", value = c("blue", "orange", "grey"), k = 3) %>%
  set("leaves_pch", 19) %>%
  set("nodes_cex", 0.7) %>%
  plot(axes = FALSE)

colored_bars(colors = my_colors, dend = mtcars_dend, rowLabels = "am")

Figure 5.3: mtcars dendrogram (3 clusters) with a transmission-type colour bar

There is no obvious pattern — automatic and manual cars are mixed across all three clusters. The clustering is driven entirely by mpg, cyl, and disp, and there is no guarantee those happen to align with any other variable we might be curious about.

6 Clustering variables instead of observations

So far we clustered observations (cars). We can just as easily cluster variables, to see which ones carry similar information. The natural measure of “closeness” between two variables is correlation: a correlation of \(+1\) means one variable is an increasing linear function of the other (as close as two variables can be); \(-1\) means a decreasing linear relationship (as far apart as possible). Since distances are usually smallest for the closest objects, we convert correlation to a distance with \(1 - \text{correlation}\).

my_mtcars <- mtcars %>% select(mpg, cyl, disp)
dd <- as.dist(1 - cor(my_mtcars))
plot(hclust(dd, method = "complete"), main = "Correlation cluster", xlab = "", sub = "")

Figure 6.1: Clustering mpg, cyl, disp by correlation distance 

1 - cor(my_mtcars)
##           mpg        cyl       disp
## mpg  0.000000 1.85216196 1.84755138
## cyl  1.852162 0.00000000 0.09796713
## disp 1.847551 0.09796713 0.00000000

cyl and disp are far closer to each other (correlation distance \(\approx 0.10\)) than either is to mpg (distance \(\approx 1.85\)) — makes sense, since bigger, more powerful engines (more cylinders, more displacement) go hand in hand, while fuel economy moves in the opposite direction from both. The same idea extends to every numeric variable in the dataset:

dd_all <- as.dist(1 - cor(mtcars))
plot(hclust(dd_all, method = "complete"), main = "All variables, correlation cluster",
    xlab = "", sub = "")

Figure 6.2: Clustering all mtcars variables by correlation distance

7 Customising and comparing dendrograms

The dendextend package’s set() function restyles any part of a dendrogram — branches, nodes, or leaves — and can colour branches by cutting the tree into \(k\) groups:

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(7, 2, 2, 2))

mtcars_dend %>%
  set("branches_col", "grey") %>% set("branches_lwd", 3) %>%
  set("labels_col", "orange") %>% set("labels_cex", 0.8) %>%
  plot(main = "Custom colours")

mtcars_dend %>%
  set("labels_col", value = c("skyblue", "orange", "grey"), k = 3) %>%
  set("branches_k_color", value = c("skyblue", "orange", "grey"), k = 3) %>%
  plot(main = "k = 3 clusters")

Figure 7.1: Styled branches/labels (left) and k = 3 colouring (right) 

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

rect.dendrogram() draws a box around each of the \(k\) clusters directly on the plot, which is often clearer than colour alone:

par(mar = c(9, 1, 1, 1))
mtcars_dend %>%
  set("labels_col", value = c("skyblue", "orange", "grey"), k = 3) %>%
  set("branches_k_color", value = c("skyblue", "orange", "grey"), k = 3) %>%
  plot(axes = FALSE)
rect.dendrogram(mtcars_dend, k = 3, lty = 5, lwd = 0, x = 1,
               col = rgb(0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.1))

Figure 7.2: Highlighting 3 clusters with rectangles

7.1 Comparing two linkage methods with a tanglegram

Since the choice of linkage can change the result, it’s useful to compare two dendrograms directly. A tanglegram places two trees side by side and draws connecting lines between matching leaves — straight, parallel lines mean the two methods agree; crossing lines highlight where they disagree.

mtcars %>% select(mpg, cyl, disp) %>% dist() %>%
  hclust(method = "complete") %>% as.dendrogram() -> mtcars_complete
mtcars %>% select(mpg, cyl, disp) %>% dist() %>%
  hclust(method = "average") %>% as.dendrogram() -> mtcars_average

dl <- dendlist(
  mtcars_average %>%
    set("labels_col", value = c("skyblue", "orange", "grey"), k = 3) %>%
    set("branches_lty", 1) %>%
    set("branches_k_color", value = c("skyblue", "orange", "grey"), k = 3),
  mtcars_complete %>%
    set("labels_col", value = c("skyblue", "orange", "grey"), k = 3) %>%
    set("branches_lty", 1) %>%
    set("branches_k_color", value = c("skyblue", "orange", "grey"), k = 3)
)

tanglegram(dl, common_subtrees_color_lines = FALSE, highlight_distinct_edges = TRUE,
          highlight_branches_lwd = FALSE, margin_inner = 7, lwd = 2)

Figure 7.3: Average linkage (left) vs. complete linkage (right), mtcars

The same approach works for any pair of clusterings on any dataset — e.g. comparing average vs. complete linkage on the (scaled) USArrests data — simply build two dendrograms and pass them to dendlist().

7.2 Alternative layouts

ape::as.phylo() converts a dendrogram into a “phylogenetic tree” object, unlocking circular layouts that can be easier to read with many leaves:

plot(as.phylo(mtcars_complete), type = "fan")

Figure 7.4: Fan layout of the mtcars complete-linkage dendrogram 

plot(as.phylo(mtcars_complete), type = "radial")

Figure 7.5: Radial layout of the same dendrogram

8 Summary

  • Clustering is unsupervised: there is no known answer to validate against, unlike every classification/regression method earlier in the course.
  • Hierarchical (agglomerative) clustering repeatedly merges whichever two clusters are currently closest, until everything is in a single cluster; the process is visualised as a dendrogram.
  • Two choices drive the result: the distance between individual points (Euclidean, Manhattan, …) and the linkage rule for distance between clusters (single/complete/average/centroid) — these can disagree, so it is worth checking more than one.
  • The dendrogram’s vertical axis marks when a merge happened, not the distance between the final clusters.
  • As with every other distance-based method in this course, standardise variables on different scales before clustering observations.
  • Clustering variables (rather than observations) using \(1 - \text{correlation}\) as a distance is a quick way to spot redundant or closely related predictors.
Concept Function Notes
Pairwise distances dist() Euclidean by default; method = "manhattan" etc. available
Hierarchical clustering hclust(d, method = ...) linkage: "single", "complete", "average", "centroid"
Convert to dendrogram as.dendrogram() supports [[ ]] indexing to inspect sub-branches
Style a dendrogram dendextend::set(...) colour/style branches, nodes, leaves; cut into k groups
Highlight k clusters rect.dendrogram(dend, k = ...) draws boxes around each cluster
Compare two dendrograms dendextend::tanglegram(dendlist(d1, d2)) crossing lines = disagreement
Circular layouts ape::as.phylo(dend) + plot(type = "fan"/"radial") easier to read with many leaves
Cluster variables as.dist(1 - cor(data)) groups variables that carry similar information

Key takeaway: hierarchical clustering asks no question about a response variable — it only asks “which observations (or variables) look alike?” — which makes it flexible and exploratory, but also means there is no built-in way to check whether the groups it finds are “correct”: that judgement is left to the analyst and to how well the clusters line up with domain knowledge.
